feedback.text: Dear Members of the European Commission, With the ongoing consultation on the Digital Fairness Act, I would like to share my concerns and recommendation regarding the protection of consumers in the video game sector. Video games today are not only a form of entertainment, but also a digital environment in which millions of Europeans, especially young people, are active on a daily basis. Unfortunately, players often face manipulative design practices such as loot boxes, addictive game mechanisms and opaque in-game purchases. These practices not only undermine consumer confidence, but can also lead to financial damage and problematic behaviour. I would also like to draw attention to the ‘Stop Killing Games’ initiative, a European Citizens’ Initiative that has now collected more than one million signatures. The action calls for regulation to prevent digital games, for which consumers have paid for, simply disappearing when publishers decide to pull the plug. The initiative advocates the right of players to maintain access to their purchased games, for example via offline modes or self-hosted servers. I therefore ask the Commission to include in the Digital Fairness Act explicit provisions that: 1. Require transparency on the true value of in-game purchases and virtual currencies. 2. Prohibit addictive and misleading design practices, such as loot boxes with no clear indication of chance or pay-to-win mechanisms. 3. Provide specific protection to minors, who are particularly vulnerable to these practices. 4. Encourage mandatory age verification and parental control in digital purchases within games. 5. Ensure access to purchased games, even after stopping online support, in line with the requirements of the Stop Killing Games initiative. By explicitly including video games in the Digital Fairness Act, the EU can send a strong signal that digital innovation must go hand in hand with consumer protection and ethical design. Yours sincerely,
